INF10025Data Management and AnalyticsLecture 8 – Part 1
Modelling
M:M relationships
SQL: Outer Joins, SubQueries,
Unions, Views

Many to Many relationships
Consider a M:M relationship
This M:M is perfectly valid in an ERD.
The relational model problem:
The Relational Model cannot directly represent a Many to Many (M:M) relationship
Instead this M:M must be converted to two M:1 relationships

Convert    M:M   to   2 * M:1
The solution is to convert the single M:M relationship into two many-to-one relationships.
Erase the M:M
Draw a weak entity in its place with appropriate name.
Draw two M:1 relationships.
The Many end of each relationship attaches to the weak entity
Underline the relationship names

Many to Many expansion
What is the identifier of the Enrolment entity?
Enrolment has no attributes of its own
Enrolment is a weak entity so at least part of it's identifier is borrowed
The relationship 'makes' is underlined.
So borrow the identifier of Student
The relationship 'for a' is underlined.
So borrow the identifier of Subject
The identifier of the Enrolment is StuNo + SubCode

Many to Many expansion
NOTICE:
No restrictions onsubjects-per-student
If we add more subjects, then there in no change to the table designs

Intersection Entity
When a M:M is expanded, the new weak entity is known as an Intersection Entity
An Intersection Entity
Has no additional attributes
Is the result of expanding a M:M relationship (or Multi valued attribute)
Intersection Entity

Unexpanded M:M relationships.
Unexpanded M:M relationships.
Advantages:
The weak intersection entity is hidden, therefore fewer entities.
Fewer entities means fewer relationships.
Results is a less cluttered diagram which is easier to read.
Relationship names have more useful descriptions
Disadvantages:
More difficult to determine how many tables will be generatedvia schema conversion.
Weak entity is hidden, so detail is lost.

Many to Many – not expanded
Sometimes ERDs are drawn as a form of documentation rather than as an aid to database design.
Read / used by non database/IT people
Both of the following ERDs are identical in the information that they provide
Which of the following two ERDs is easier to:
Read?
Understand?
Convert to Relational Schema?
How many tables are generated by the 2nd ER Diagram?

Many to Many – not expanded
Consider this ERD. Is it easy to  Read?   Understand?   Convert to Schema?
Most non-database peoplecan easily understand thisERD, after 30-60 seconds oftuition about symbols

Many to Many - expanded
Consider this ERD. Is it easy to  Read?   Understand?   Convert to Schema?
Most non-database peoplefind it difficult to understand
this ERD.
Symbols are more confusing.
What is the differencebetween a student and a
student society member?
Why are they two differententities?

Modelling: Designing your own ERDs
This lecture we will examine
how to read and interpret a business narrative
create and draw an ERD based on the narrative
You will be asked to create your own ERDs
In tutorials
In submission tasks

Steps involved in ERD design
Step 1 – Identify all Strong Entities and all Attributes
Step 2 – Assign attributes to the Strong Entities
Step 3 – Identify relationships
Step 4 – Determine cardinality and participation constraints
Step 5 – Attach remaining attributes to relationships
Step 6 – Expand M:M relationships with attributes
Step 7 – Attach attributes to appropriate entities or relationships
Step 8 – Determine identifiers
Step 9 – Test your solution.
Create test data.
Read the ERD aloud.
Navigate the diagram.
Think of how SQL queries would be written when theERD is transformed into a database solution.

Identifying Entities
ERD Entities:
Each entity must have many instances
"The Business" therefore is not an entity
Singular noun (not verb).  Written in Uppercase
Use nouns specific to the Business
Are you dealing with a  customer or client or patron?
May be a Strong Entity identified by its own attributes
eg. StudentId
May be a Weak Entity identified (wholly or partly) by attributes that are ‘borrowed’ from other entities
eg. Enrolment

Identifying Attributes
ERD Attributes
Each attribute is Identified by a single word or phrase
use underscores or capitalisation to separate words
Does not have properties of its own
if it does, then its probably an entity
An attribute is dependent on the identifier of the entity
The value of StuAddress is dependant on the value of the identifier StudentId

Glenferrie Toy Library Narrative
Sample Business Narrative:
Glenferrie Toy Library has many Patrons. A Patrons have a name and a gender. Each new Patron is assigned a sequential patron number. The library has a number of toys.
Every toy in the library has a unique toy number.
Each toy has a description and year of acquisition.
Each time a toy is loaned to a patron, the date of the loan is recorded. When the toy is returned, the returned date is recorded and the no. of days borrowed is determined.
Each loan is for a single toy.
Glenferrie Toy Library
Patron ID Card
Patron No: 12345678

Identify Entities and Attributes
Identify key elements such as Entities & Attributes
Element
Circle best alternative

Step 1 – Identify Strong Entities & Attributes
From the business narrative, identify Attributes and Strong Entities
PATRON
TOY
O Gender
O Description
O PatronNo
O YearAquired
O ToyNo
O DateBorrowed
O Firstname
O DateReturned
O Surname
This may seem like an un-necessary step, but it can be useful when dealing with an industry where many terms are foreign & unfamiliar

Step 2 – Attach attributes to entities
Attach attributes to Strong entities and underline identifiers

The DateBorrowed Attribute
To which entity (if any), should the DateBorrowed attribute be attached?

Consider an instance of a Patron
What does this diagram mean?
PatronNo is the identifier.
E.g.  53
How many surnames does this patron 53 have?
ONE     E.g. Sofia Vegara
How many genders does this patron 53 have?
ONE     E.g. Female

The DateBorrowed Attribute
So let's consider the DateBorrowed attribute:
What if it was added to the Patron Entity?
What would this diagram now mean?
PatronNo is the identifier.
Example:  53
How many DateBorrowed values are allowed for patron 53?
ONE    e.g. 1 June
This does not make sense!   You would expect that a Patron
Could borrow many times over many weeks.
You would expect a patron to have many DateBorrowed values
Therefore we can conclude that: DateBorrowed does not belong to the Patron Entity

The DateBorrowed Attribute
SO let's consider the DateBorrowed attribute:
What if it was added to the Toy Entity?
What would this diagram now mean?
ToyNo is the identifier.
E.g.  T27
How many DateBorrowed values are allowed for toy T27?
ONE         E.g. 15 Sept
This does not make sense!    You would expect that a Toy
Will be borrowed many times over many weeks.
You would expect a toy to have many DateBorrowed values
Therefore we can conclude that: DateBorrowed does not belong to the Toy Entity

Step 2 – Attach attributes to entities
Some attributes simply don't belong to any of the strong entities.
DateBorrowed
DateReturned
Leave these attributes alone.
They will be dealt with in future steps of this process.

Step 3 - Identifying Relationships
Entities have relationships with other entities
That's why they're called Entity Relationship Diagrams!
Use relevant verbs
The relationship must be worth recording
The relationship must persistent, not transient
"The student enrolled in a subject"
rather than
"The student read the subject course finder web page"
Watch for "abstract" nouns
"A student makes an enrolment"
is the same as
"A student enrols in a subject".
You should be able to verbalize the relationship
eg. You can say "A Subject has many Enrolments"

Step 3
Identify any relationships between entities
Easy in this diagram as there are only two entities!
"A patron borrows toys"
So draw a line between the entities
DateBorrowed
DateReturned

Step 4
Determine cardinality and participation constraints.
DateBorrowed
DateReturned

Step 5
Attach ALL remaining attributes to entities or relationships.
Ask yourself, does it make sense for the attributes to 'belong' to the relationship
After this step, all attributes must be attached to an entity or to a relationship.

Step 6 – expand M:M with attributes
If your diagram now contains a M:M with attached attributes, then expand that M:M relationship
Expand M:M relationships that have attributes
DateBorrowed
DateReturned
Sometimes, during this process, you may discover more underlying M:M relationships, so you can't simply assume that the Dates will both belong to the Loan Entity.

Step 7 – reattach attributes
Consider each outstanding attribute
Does each Loan have a single DateBorrowed?   (Yes)
Does each Loan have a single DateReturned  (Yes. It may be NULL)
If an attribute belongs to an Entity, add it to the diagram

Identifiers…
What is the identifier of Loan?
If you are not sure, then a good approach is to create a relational schema and use some sample data…
